channel.title: Abhidharma on Early Reflections
channel.description: Recent content in Abhidharma on Early Reflections

item.title: Note on Immanence (or, Conventional Truth Without Ultimate Reality)
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description:
In this post I’d like to think a bit about the distinction between ultimate and conventional truth, and to consider to what extent these different kinds of truth should be taken as corresponding to ultimate and conventional levels of reality. This is a doctrinal issue which receives different treatments in different places, and reflecting on it can help clarify some of the more paradoxical teachings in the later tradition.

In the Abhidharma, ultimate and conventional levels of truth map onto ultimate and conventional levels of reality in a straightforward way. According to this framework the only ultimately real entities are dharmas, and ultimate truth is just the accurate description of these dharmas. The objects and phenomena picked out in conventional designations (tables, trees, emotions, selves, thoughts) lack ultimate reality because they are mere aggregates, perceptual and conceptual fictions overlaying the ever-flowing stream of dharmas. Nonetheless we may still speak of a difference between truth and falsehood at the conventional level, because some of these fictions are useful and others are not. The utility of conventional designations is unpacked in terms of whether or not they line up with the clusters of dharmas on which they are imposed. Ultimate truths explain what counts as conventional truth, and facts at the ultimate level underwrite the possibility of error at the conventional level.
item.content:encoded:
In this post I’d like to think a bit about the distinction between ultimate and conventional truth, and to consider to what extent these different kinds of truth should be taken as corresponding to ultimate and conventional levels of reality. This is a doctrinal issue which receives different treatments in different places, and reflecting on it can help clarify some of the more paradoxical teachings in the later tradition.

In the Abhidharma, ultimate and conventional levels of truth map onto ultimate and conventional levels of reality in a straightforward way. According to this framework the only ultimately real entities are dharmas, and ultimate truth is just the accurate description of these dharmas. The objects and phenomena picked out in conventional designations (tables, trees, emotions, selves, thoughts) lack ultimate reality because they are mere aggregates, perceptual and conceptual fictions overlaying the ever-flowing stream of dharmas. Nonetheless we may still speak of a difference between truth and falsehood at the conventional level, because some of these fictions are useful and others are not. The utility of conventional designations is unpacked in terms of whether or not they line up with the clusters of dharmas on which they are imposed. Ultimate truths explain what counts as conventional truth, and facts at the ultimate level underwrite the possibility of error at the conventional level.

This account has two components. The first is an ontology: dharmas are real, everything else is mental projection which may or may not line up with what’s going on for real. The second is the idea that truth is accurate description, in particular that ultimate truth is accurate description of ultimate reality. These two components add up to a fairly standard kind of reductionism, in which the ultimate reality of the dharmas serves as a non-conventional (or transcendent) ground securing the objectivity of the conventional world of appearances.

There are many ways of approaching the break with the Abhidharma represented by Nagarjuna, but one fruitful way in is to view it as a radical rethinking of this relationship between truth and reality. Of course Nagarjuna, following the Perfection of Wisdom literature, rejected the ultimate reality of dharmas, instead establishing universal emptiness as the ultimate truth. But it is essential to this account that emptiness not be taken as a competing description of ultimate reality. The claim of universal emptiness should be seen rather as a rejection of any attempt to give a positive account of an ultimate reality which underwrites the world of conventional appearance. The ‘emptiness of emptiness’—the climax of Nagarjuna’s analysis1—makes clear that this is not intended as a new account of ultimate reality, but a rejection of the assumption that conventional truth must be underwritten by an account of the way reality is in itself.

This is sometimes called the ‘semantic interpretation’ of Nagarjuna’s theory of the two truths, which Mark Siderits has glossed as the idea that ‘the ultimate truth is that there is no ultimate truth’2. This rather glib formulation can be put less paradoxically by saying that the ultimate truth is that there is no ultimate reality. The natural question arising here is, what then happens to conventional truth? The Abdhidharma finds a transcendent ground of conventional truth in the ultimate reality of dharmas. Nagarjuna appears to reject the very idea of a transcendent ground, so what, we might ask, could possibly make sense of the idea that some judgements couched in conventional terms are true while others are not? What makes a useful fiction useful, if not that it tracks some feature of things as they really are? What accounts for the objective content of conventional appearances? If no good answers to these questions can be found, then the charge that Nagarjuna’s position entails a form of nihilism would seem to be vindicated.

One answer, which seems to be standard in the tradition, is that this notion of a ‘useful fiction’ is just where the chain of explanation bottoms out. If the Abhidharma wants to explain the utility of conventional belief in terms of ultimate truths about dharmas, then the proposed alternative is that we just explain conventional truth in terms of conventional utility. It is common to see it written, for example, that conventional entities are like illusions. The only difference between ‘real’ water and a mirage in the desert is that the former quenches thirst while the latter does not. Some fictions are useful, others are not—there simply is nothing else to say about their truth, since utility is all that truth amounts to.3

The problem with this is that the concept of utility doesn’t seem to contain the resources needed to pin down what we mean by truth. No matter how useful a belief is, I can always imagine a situation in which it is false despite this utility. Indeed, I can even entertain the possibility that at least some of my own beliefs are both useful and false. And if this is so then when I take it that some judgement is true, I cannot simply mean by this that it would be useful to believe. A proponent of the view might object that it is not intended to provide an analysis of the meaning of truth, but just that considerations of utility—including, perhaps, indirect considerations such as the development of our cognitive faculties as evolutionary adaptations—are what ultimately explain what it is we are doing when we call a belief true. But even so, on such an account it will always be possible to construct scenarios in which utility and truth come apart. Ultimately this route seems unable to provide a non-relativistic account of conventional truth, in which case it cannot claim to have secured the objectivity of the conventional and countered the charge of nihilism.

This brief sketch of what goes wrong with a utility theory gestures to a more general concern. If we reject the idea that the objective content of conventional appearance is determined by a transcendent ground, yet want to hold on to the idea that there is such a content, then we are left searching for something like an ‘immanent ground’. In practice this means that rather than explaining truth in terms of some correspondence between our conventional beliefs and the way things really (i.e. ultimately) are, we will have to explain it in terms which remain within the bounds of the conventional. But this is always going to create problems. Given any conventional belief and any set of conventional considerations which determine it as true, it will always be possible to produce further conventional considerations which undermine that determination. In essence, this is a version of what in philosophy of science is known as the Duhem-Quine thesis: given any set of observations it is always possible to construct multiple conflicting theories which adequately explain them. The whole reason to posit an ultimate reality—a way things are in themselves, independently of our conventional designations—is that it serves as a final tribunal before which disputes between competing conventional accounts can be settled. Without such a tribunal it seems the proliferation of conflicting conventional ‘truths’ can always proceed unchecked.

If there can be neither a transcendent nor an immanent ground of conventional truth, does that not mean that the tradition downstream from Nagarjuna cannot make good on its claim that conventional truth survives the analysis of universal emptiness? I think there’s a move that can be made here. It is not one I’ve ever encountered in a Buddhist context, but it is a key idea in German Idealism, and in Hegelian dialectics in particular. The trick is to exploit a certain asymmetry in the notions of truth and error. When interpreting an ancient text, for example, we may concede that it is impossible to ever reach its ‘true’ meaning. In this sense it is essentially open to reinterpretation. But this does not render it meaningless, and what seems to underlie this intuition is the fact that while we may never be able to settle the question of its unique true meaning, it is perfectly possible to identify wrong or implausible readings. In this respect, while interpretations can always proliferate this cannot be described as an unchecked proliferation—it is governed by an objective logic according to which erroneous readings are uncovered and discarded. Without ever making reference to a true meaning that transcends interpretation, the structure of a space of possible meanings is progressively articulated in the ongoing dialogue between conflicting interpretations.

It may not be possible to locate an immanent ground of truth, but perhaps all that is needed to avert nihilism is something more like an immanent condition of error. In Hegel’s system this immanent condition is given by the notion of internal contradiction. We do not determine what is true in our conventional experience by comparing it with an ultimate reality—this latter is a fiction (or in more modern terms: the evidence is always contaminated by theory). Instead what happens is that the experience of error manifests through internal contradictions in our total conventional theory of reality. Contradictions are then resolved through a process of reinterpretation in which the error is integrated (typically by uncovering and discarding the implicit assumptions that produced the contradiction, and/or by producing new interpretive hypotheses). There is no ultimate reality—a transcendent ground, a language in which the world speaks. Reality has a radically open texture. Yet this need not lead to the nihilistic conclusion that all conventional theories are arbitrary. Reality is always open to reinterpretation, but the integration of error provides this process with an objective constraint in the generation and resolution of internal contradiction.

Clearly this departs from accounts of emptiness offered by the tradition. Yet it sometimes seems to me that these accounts can vacillate between a ‘thin’ conception of emptiness which makes no difference at all to our conventional experience, undermining the claim that universal emptiness is a radical proposition with liberating potential, and a ‘thicker’ conception which, while certainly radical, never adequately responds to the charge of nihilism. While the quick sketch above leaves many questions unanswered, it seems to me to at least gesture in the direction of a resolution to this problem.

  1. See chapter 24 of Nagarjuna’s Mūlamadhyamakakārikā, “Analysis of the Four Noble Truths.” [1] ↩︎

  2. See Siderits’ 2007 book Buddhism as Philosophy, especially the chapter on the two truths [2]. The semantic interpretation is not uncontroversial—see, e.g., Giuseppe Ferraro’s criticism [3]—but I won’t consider that dispute here. The rest of this post will not mean much to anyone who subscribes to a realist interpretation of Nagarjuna’s theory of the two truths. ↩︎

  3. Something like this seems to be expressed by Chandrakirti when he says that “Everything the six unimpaired senses grasp and perceive within everyday experience of the world is true from the perspective of the world; the rest the world itself defines as false.” Madhyamakāvatāra 6.25 [4] ↩︎

item.title: The Three Turnings in Classical Yogacara
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: One of the key differences between the early Buddhist and later Mahayana teachings is the way they each portray the relationship of the path to its destination. The early teachings stress that in its natural state the mind is obscured by very real afflictions, and the path is accordingly presented as the effort to draw up a map of the afflicted mind and to then use it to systematically cut away the fetters at the root of suffering. Nirvana is achieved only once this active and strenuous process has been completed, a world-transcending state which marks a definitive exit from samsara.
item.content:encoded:
One of the key differences between the early Buddhist and later Mahayana teachings is the way they each portray the relationship of the path to its destination. The early teachings stress that in its natural state the mind is obscured by very real afflictions, and the path is accordingly presented as the effort to draw up a map of the afflicted mind and to then use it to systematically cut away the fetters at the root of suffering. Nirvana is achieved only once this active and strenuous process has been completed, a world-transcending state which marks a definitive exit from samsara.

In contrast, Mahayana traditions tend to stress that the mind is always already awakened, and construe the path as a process of coming to realise this. They are suspicious of modes of practice which treat the afflictions as substantially real, worrying that in doing to so they only reinforce the reifying tendency which inhibits realisation of the Buddha-nature which is already there. Instead they recommend practices based on not-doing, typically non-dual or open awareness practices which aim at a certain ‘abiding in thusness’ without conceptualisation (tantric practices depart from this in some ways, but inhabit the same overall framework). Samsara already is nirvana—your job is to notice that.

The Buddha’s own comments can be ambiguous on this point, as for example when he likens the path to the purification of gold. On the one hand the gold is already there. But on the other hand so are the impurities, and their removal implies a process of real change. How different are these two presentations, really? The question feels live for me, as I currently seem to be converging on a mode of practice which is essentially neo-Theravada insight meditation, but with a view of the Dharma which is informed primarily by Mahayana philosophy. On the one hand I find the detailed and systematic approach of insight meditation generates enthusiasm and curiosity in me in way that non-dual practices simply do not, at least at this stage of my practice. Even if you accept that the aim is to stop doing something, rather than to do something, you still need to have a grasp on what exactly it is that you’re aiming to stop doing. Or to use another of the Buddha’s similes, if the Dharma is likened to a raft then you can accept that the raft is just a tool for getting to the other shore, not an end in itself, while also acknowledging that you do in fact need a raft and can’t just teleport across the river.

Yet at the same time I remain sceptical of world-transcending interpretations of nirvana, and do agree that this model generates meta-ethical problems that can’t be resolved without embracing a more thorough-going philosophy of emptiness and something like the Bodhisattva ideal (though I’d perhaps add the caveat that exactly how these innovations solve those problems is far less obvious than generally acknowledged).

Squaring this circle has been one of the main factors driving me to explore Yogacara more deeply. Following on from Ben Connelley’s book on Vasubandhu’s Thirty Verses, which I discussed previously, I’ve been reading William Waldron’s superb Making Sense of Mind-Only [1], which is significantly more comprehensive and historical in scope. Like Connelly, Waldron rejects the interpretation of classical Yogacara (which includes the Yogacara sutras along with the classic expositions of Vasubandhu and Asanga in the 4th or 5th century) as a metaphysical idealism. Instead he sees it as a form of cognitive constructivism, more concerned with how experience arises as a function of the interaction between our environment and our mental faculties than with fundamental ontology.

Of particular interest here is the Yogacara teaching on the “three turnings of the wheel of Dharma,” a framework through which Yogacara synthesises the detailed psychological maps of earlier Buddhism with the new Mahayana emptiness teachings found in the Perfection of Wisdom sutras and their explication by Nagarjuna. The first turning refers to both the early Buddhist teachings as presented in the Pali discourses, and their systematisation in the various Abhidharma schools that followed.

According to the Abhidharma analysis all experience can be broken down into constitutive elements called (somewhat confusingly) dharmas, point-like moments of awareness constantly flashing in and out of existence. All the normal objects of our experience—medium-sized dry goods, thoughts, sensations, our sense of self—are just higher-level patternings of these momentary dharmas, appearing with a false solidity in roughly the same way that moving objects appear on a film screen despite being constituted by a rapid sequence of static frames. One of the features of many Abhidharma systems is that the dharmas are the only entities considered to ultimately exist, while everything else exists only in some derivative sense.

The second turning refers to the Mahayana emptiness teachings, and its distinctive innovation is that it rejects the ultimate reality of dharmas. A consequence of this stance is that it flattens the difference in status between the dharmas and aggregate phenomena, which are now all viewed as equally empty of essence or substantial reality. One lesson that could be taken from this, which later comes to define the Madhyamaka school, is that Buddhist theorists should stop trying to construct positive accounts of the functioning of the mind and reorient themselves toward the deconstruction of any and all views that treat—explicitly or implicitly—some entity or class of entities as non-empty.

While Madhyamaka could be described as an anti-metaphysics, it remains in a metaphysical register in the sense that its purely negative style of reasoning aims to deconstruct the fallacy of reification that underlies all positive metaphysical views, a fallacy it takes to be the primary barrier to liberation. In light of this it is perhaps not surprising that Madhyamaka philosophers like Candrakirti, writing several hundred years after Asanga and Vasubandhu, often construed Yogacara as a metaphysical idealism only to then accuse it of reifying the mind.

Waldron argues that Yogacara is better seen as a different kind of response to the emptiness teachings, one which can first be approached by considering the limitations of Madhyamaka. In restricting itself to purely deconstructive discourse, Madhyamaka deprives itself of the resources needed to answer certain kinds of questions. For example, Madhyamaka agrees that there is such a thing as conventional truth and hence that there is a difference between conventional truth and conventional falsehood. But at the same time it seems to preclude the possiblity of our ever being able to say what this difference consists in. The Madhyamika seems committed to a kind of quietism about the nature of conventional truth—the best they could say is that if you want to know what mathematical truth is, ask a mathematician; if you want to know what historical truth is, ask a historian; but there is no way of getting at an overarching, domain-neutral theory of conventional truth which the univocity of the concept seems to demand. This is not inconsistent, but it is certainly unsatisfying. The Madhyamika might say that the difference is just that some conventional beliefs are useful while others are not, but a statement like that remains vacuous in the absence of an account of what it is that might make a conventional belief useful in general. And it is exactly that kind of account that Madhyamaka forbids itself from offering.

Furthermore, from the point of view of liberation the issue is not really that we believe phenomena to non-empty. The issue is that we perceive them to be so, and this may be the case regardless of what we believe. If we are to free ourselves of our ingrained perception of the world as infused with illusory essence, what we’d really like is a substantial account of why it is that our mental faculties automatically impute essences to phenomena which do not in fact possess them. Once again, here Madhyamaka can only maintain a principled silence. But for Yogacara this is the key question, and what allows it to take it up as a positive program is precisely its reframing of the emptiness teachings in cognitive rather than metaphysical terms. This is the move which defines the third turning of the wheel of Dharma.

For Yogacarins, then, the relationship to the Abhidharma looks like this. As per the emptiness teachings they reject the ultimate reality of the dharmas, but they do not take this as reason to reject the Abhidharma framework as a whole. Instead they reinterpret the dharmas as something more like epistemological primitives than ontological primitives—as building blocks of a theory of mind rather than building blocks of reality. This allows them to use those aspects of the Abhidharma which successfully explain our mental patterns while augmenting them with extra elements which could help to explain those aspects of mind which the Abhidharma system struggled to accommodate. For instance in another of its signature teachings, Yogacara posits an unconscious layer of mental processing to account for memory and karmic continuity, neither of which can easily be made sense of in a system in which the only components are momentary, sequential, and manifestly present dharmas.

What all this points to is a way of integrating the Mahayana emptiness teachings with a form of practice that is continuous with, rather than divergent from, insight practices rooted in the substantial content of the first turning psychology. Without seeing these as descriptions of substantially real afflictions one can nevertheless actively engage them as necessary tools—as in the simile of the raft—in coming to see exactly how the illusion of substantiality is staged, and which can be discarded once they have served their purpose.
